steps.text: Ensure the static dilution system includes a calibrated pressure transducer or pressure gauge and a manifold.
Construct connection tubing for the static dilution system from chromatographic-grade or silicon-ceramic–coated stainless steel.
Construct manifolds from chromatographic-grade or silicon-ceramic–coated stainless steel, borosilicate, or quartz glass.
Recalibrate the static gas dilution system once per calendar year using NIST-traceable primary pressure gauge.
Report the results of the calibration whenever the dilution system is used.
Verify the gas dilution system to be non-biasing under HFC zero air and known standards at least once per calendar year for each reactive target compound.
Flow zero air through all applicable tubing and manifold and verify it is not detectable for the target compounds.
Flow a known standard within the calibration range for each target compound through all applicable tubing and manifold into the standard canister or vessel.
Verify the standard is not biased by ± 15% of the concentrations in the reference sample.
Use only NIST-certified or NIST-traceable calibration standards and reagents.
Use standards and reagents within their expiration period.
Evaluate working-level standards prepared in canisters within 30 days of preparation.
Demonstrate that the analytical instrumentation is non-biasing under Method 327 known standards.
Analyze a humidified (40 to 50% RH) reference standard in duplicate containing all target compounds at approximately 100 to 500 pptv each.
Ensure that the target compounds in the sample collected are within ± 15% of the expected concentrations.
Consult the instrument manufacturer if you identify problems with bias.
Demonstrate that the auto sampling equipment is non-biasing under zero-air.
Test all ports, transfer lines, and connections of the autosampler after calibration.
Connect humidified (40 to 50% RH) HCF zero air to each port and verify that the concentration for each target compound is < 20 pptv or undetected.
Challenge each port of the autosampler with a reference standard to verify that the autosampler is not causing bias.
Ensure that the concentration of each target compound is within ± 15% of the theoretical concentration of the reference standard.
Ensure sampling canisters meet the performance criteria for fenceline measurements.
Use specially prepared canisters at least 6-liters volume in size that are suitable for trace gas analysis of the target compounds.
Handle canisters with care to ensure that the interior canister surface is not compromised, the valve-to-canister connection remains intact, and weld integrity is maintained.
Do not use any canister with dents or compromised welds.
Maintain a record of the results for all canisters used for this method.
Qualify each canister as being acceptably leak-tight to ensure sample validity.
Conduct the canister leak check either by evacuating the canister to high vacuum ≤ 0.0067 kPa absolute or pressurizing the canister with clean fill gas to > 203 kPa absolute.
After establishing the target pressure in the canister, close the valve and attach a vacuum/pressure gauge.
Open the valve and record the initial pressure reading.

preconditions.text: Qualify new canisters before initial use and qualify all canisters used for sampling at least annually.

constraints.text: Ensure canisters are handled carefully to avoid damage.
Do not use canisters that have dents or compromised welds.

steps.text: Analyze the field duplicate during the same analytical sequence as the accompanying field sample.
Flag associated results to indicate if the RPD indicates poor method precision.
Analyze at least one replicate analysis for each set of field-collected samples.
Flag associated results to indicate if the RPD indicates poor method precision.
Perform analytical zero-air verification by analyzing humidified zero air.
Connect the clean humidified gas sample to the pre concentrator.
Verify that the analytical instrument and all connections are sufficiently clean.
Ensure analysis shows detected target compounds in the zero-air challenge sample are at response levels < 20 pptv or not detected.
Take steps to remove contamination attributable to the analytical instrumentation by following the manufacturer’s instructions.

preconditions.text: Perform the test of instrumentation at installation prior to initial use of the instrument.

steps.text: Remove from service.
Repair canister connections and/or valve.
Perform canister zero-air verification test to determine that they are acceptably clean over the course of 7 days.
Clean and retest canisters that fail the zero-air verification.
Remove canisters from service that cannot pass the zero-air verification after the cleaning process.
Perform Instrument blank (IB) analysis of an injection where no sample or standard is introduced to the preconcentrator.
Repeat IB analysis.
Bakeout preconcentrator system and repeat IB analysis.
Replace contaminated tubing/traps as needed.
Perform Method blank (MB) analysis with canister filled with clean, humidified diluent gas.
Repeat analysis for Method blank (MB).
Reprepare the MB canister and reanalyze.
Check the system for leaks.
Prepare Calibration blank (CB) with clean, humidified diluent gas sourced through the standard preparation dilution system.
Reanalyze Calibration blank (CB).
Reprepare CB and ICAL canister standards.

preconditions.text: Prior to ICAL and at the beginning of an analytical sequence, ensure that the Instrument blank (IB) analysis demonstrates that each target VOC’s concentration must be < 20 pptv.

Prior to and following the ICAL and daily following the IB/BFB and prior to the initial daily CCV/SSCV, ensure that the Method blank (MB) analysis demonstrates that each target VOC’s concentration must be < 20 pptv.
With each set of calibration standard canisters, prepare one Calibration blank (CB) and analyze with each ICAL.

steps.text: Conduct a BFB tuning check for linear quadrupole or ion trap MS instruments.
Introduce 1 to 2 ng of BFB into the preconcentrator and analyze the standard using the preconcentrator parameters established.
Use the method integration and analysis parameters employed for routine analysis of standards, QC samples, and field samples.
Ensure that the BFB tuning check shows that the GC-MS system meets the mass spectral ion abundance criteria listed in Table 2 before using the system for any analysis.
Adjust the tuning of the MS or take corrective actions if the GC-MS system cannot meet the BFB tuning criteria.
Select IS compound(s) to be used for oHAP analysis.
Evaluate the IS upon receipt for the presence of contaminants that may interfere with the quantitation of target compounds.
Add the IS through a dedicated non-sample port in the preconcentrator at the same concentration for each injection.

Choose the concentration of IS added to each injection such that the peak area response for the IS compound approximates the area responses for target compounds in the lower half of the calibration curve range.
Ensure each IS compound in each sample injection is within ±2 seconds of the RT.

preconditions.text: Before conducting the BFB tuning check, ensure that it is done at least once during every 24-hour period of analyzing samples, blanks, or calibration standards.
Do not use IS gas standards that fail the MB acceptance criteria.

steps.text: Prepare a calibration curve for each target analyte by determining the relative response factor (RRF) of each concentration level.
Choose the quantitation ion for each target compound as the most abundant ion unless there is a spectral interference.
Select another abundant ion that is distinguishable from the other compounds for quantitation if a spectral interference exists.
Ensure the %RSD of the RRF for each target compound must be ≤ 30%.
Calculate the concentration for each target compound(s) at each calibration level and ensure it is within ± 30% of the theoretical concentration.
Review the sample collection information documented on the field data page and/or COC form(s) for completeness and accuracy.
Compare the canister label with the sample collection data sheet and verify that the canister and sample IDs are correct.
Measure and record the canister pressure using a calibrated vacuum/pressure gauge or transducer.
Ensure the measured canister absolute pressure is within ±3.5 kPa of that measured upon collection in the field.
Pressurize the canister with diluent gas to a pressure less than or equal to the final pressure of the standard gas canisters.
Measure the canister pressure just prior to dilution and immediately following dilution.
Calculate the canister dilution correction factor (CDCF) from the two absolute pressure readings.
Allow diluted canisters to equilibrate for a minimum of 12 hours before analysis.
Draw a measured aliquot of the whole air sample from the sample canister by vacuum.
Adjust preconcentrator instrument parameters as needed for target compounds.
Analyze samples using the same acquisition methods used for calibration.
Ensure field-collected samples and QC samples are at ambient laboratory temperature for analysis.
Use approximately the same sample aliquot volume for all samples unless dilution is required.
Verify each canister connection as leak-free through the preconcentrator prior to beginning an analytical sequence.
Connect canisters to the autosampler or sample introduction lines while maintaining the canister valves in the closed position.
Evacuate each port of the autosampler or sample introduction line and monitor for a change in pressure for 1 minute.
Implement corrective actions if a sample line fails the leak check and retest.
Evacuate all autosampler ports or sample introduction lines, open the canister valves, and document the leak check results.
Connect each sample canister to the preconcentration unit through a port and verify a leak-free connection.
Measure the sample aliquot volume for analysis accurately.
Verify the instrument calibration by analysis of a CCV and begin sample analysis.
